question: A smart navigation system displays the movement of an autonomous shuttle using a distance-time graph. Which of the following best describes what a distance-time graph represents?

options.A: How the total distance travelled by an object changes with time
options.B: How the direction of motion changes with time
options.C: How the acceleration of an object changes with distance
options.D: How the velocity of an object changes with time

answer_text: How the total distance travelled by an object changes with time

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the basic meaning of a distance-time graph before moving to graph interpretation and calculations.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph is one of the simplest graphical methods used to describe motion.

It shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes as time passes.

In this graph:

• Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis.
• Distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The graph enables us to determine whether an object is moving or stationary and whether it moves with uniform or changing speed.

Unlike a displacement-time graph, a distance-time graph does not provide information about the direction of motion because distance is a scalar quantity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what quantity is represented on the vertical axis.

A distance-time graph plots distance travelled against time.

Step 2: Consider what information the graph conveys.

It shows how the total distance covered changes with time during the journey.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option A correctly describes a distance-time graph.

Option B refers to direction of motion, which is not represented by a distance-time graph.

Option C incorrectly relates acceleration to distance.

Option D describes a velocity-time graph instead.

Step 4: Select the correct answer.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because distance is a scalar quantity and does not indicate direction of motion.
Option C is incorrect because acceleration is not represented directly on a distance-time graph.
Option D is incorrect because a graph showing velocity against time is called a velocity-time graph, not a distance-time graph.
common_misconceptions: Some students confuse a distance-time graph with a displacement-time graph.
Some believe a distance-time graph shows acceleration instead of distance travelled.
Some assume the graph indicates direction because an object is moving, but distance has no direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
For JAMB examinations, always remember:

• Distance-time graph → Distance against time.
• Gradient of a distance-time graph → Speed.
• Horizontal line → Object is at rest.
• Steeper gradient → Higher speed.
explanation.final_answer: A. How the total distance travelled by an object changes with time
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine tracking the odometer reading of a vehicle every few minutes.

A distance-time graph simply records how the total distance travelled increases as time goes on. It does not tell you the direction in which the vehicle is moving.

question:
During a digital physics lesson, a teacher displays a distance-time graph on an interactive screen. A student is asked to identify the quantities represented on the graph axes.

Which of the following correctly states the axes of a distance-time graph?

options.A: Distance on the horizontal axis and time on the vertical axis
options.B: Time on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis
options.C: Speed on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis
options.D: Time on the horizontal axis and speed on the vertical axis

answer_text: Time on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how a distance-time graph is labelled. Before interpreting any motion graph, you must know which physical quantity belongs to each axis.
explanation.principle:
A graph is a visual way of showing how one physical quantity changes with another.

In a distance-time graph:

• Time is the independent variable because it progresses naturally as an event occurs.
• Distance is the dependent variable because the distance travelled changes as time passes.

By convention, the independent variable is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while the dependent variable is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

Therefore:

• Horizontal (x) axis → Time
• Vertical (y) axis → Distance

This standard arrangement allows the gradient of the graph to represent speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantity that changes independently.

Time passes continuously regardless of the object's motion, so it is the independent variable.

Step 2: Determine the quantity that depends on time.

The distance travelled increases or remains constant as time passes, making distance the dependent variable.

Step 3: Apply the standard graphing convention.

Independent variables are plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while dependent variables are plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

Step 4: Compare the options.

Only Option B correctly places time on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it reverses the standard positions of the axes.
Option C is incorrect because speed is not one of the axes of a distance-time graph. Speed is obtained from the graph's gradient.
Option D is incorrect because a graph with time and speed on its axes is a speed-time or velocity-time graph, not a distance-time graph.
common_misconceptions: Some students interchange the horizontal and vertical axes.
Some believe speed should be plotted because motion is involved. In reality, speed is calculated from the gradient of the distance-time graph.
Some confuse a distance-time graph with a velocity-time graph because both involve time on the horizontal axis.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you encounter a distance-time graph in JAMB, first confirm the graph axes before interpreting gradients or calculating speed. Misreading the axes can lead to incorrect answers even when your calculations are correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. Time on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine watching a journey unfold. Time keeps moving forward, so it is placed along the horizontal axis. As time passes, the distance travelled changes, so distance is plotted on the vertical axis. This arrangement makes it easy to determine the speed from the graph's slope.

question: A highway traffic monitoring centre analyses a distance–time graph generated by an automated vehicle tracking system. The graph is a straight line that rises uniformly as time increases. Which physical quantity is represented by the gradient of the graph?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of what the gradient or slope of a distance–time graph represents.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The gradient of a graph describes how quickly the vertical quantity changes with the horizontal quantity.

For a distance–time graph, the gradient is calculated as:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

Therefore, the gradient of a distance–time graph represents the speed of the moving object.

A steeper straight line indicates a greater speed, while a horizontal line has zero gradient, showing that the object is at rest.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the type of graph.

The graph described is a distance–time graph.

Step 2: Recall what the gradient means.

Gradient = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

Step 3: Recognise the physical quantity.

Change in distance divided by change in time is the definition of speed.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Therefore, the gradient represents the speed of the vehicle.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because acceleration is obtained from the gradient of a velocity–time graph, not a distance–time graph.
Option C is incorrect because distance is represented by the vertical axis, not by the gradient.
Option D is incorrect because time is represented on the horizontal axis and is not obtained from the gradient.
common_misconceptions: Some candidates confuse speed with acceleration and wrongly think the gradient of every motion graph represents acceleration.
Others read the height of the graph instead of its slope when asked to interpret a graph.
Some candidates confuse the axes and assume the gradient represents distance instead of the rate at which distance changes.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, always identify the quantities plotted on the two axes before interpreting a graph. For a distance–time graph, remember the simple rule:

Gradient = Speed.

For a velocity–time graph, Gradient = Acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: A. Speed of the vehicle
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two vehicles travelling along a road. The one whose distance increases more rapidly with time is moving faster. On a distance–time graph, this appears as a steeper line. Therefore, the slope or gradient tells you the speed of the object.

question:
Engineers testing an autonomous shuttle describe its distance–time graph as follows:

The graph starts at the origin and rises as a straight line from 0 m to 200 m during the first 40 s.

Which of the following best describes the motion of the shuttle during this interval?

options.C: It remains at rest throughout the interval.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the meaning of a straight-line section on a distance–time graph.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

On such a graph:

• Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis.
• Distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.
• The gradient of the graph represents speed.

When the graph is a straight line with a constant slope, the object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. This means the speed remains constant, a condition known as uniform motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the shape of the graph.

The graph is described as a straight line rising uniformly from the origin.

Step 2: Recall the meaning of a straight-line distance–time graph.

A constant gradient indicates that the object's speed does not change with time.

Step 3: Interpret the motion.

Since the shuttle covers equal distances in equal time intervals, it is travelling with uniform speed.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly describes the motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because increasing speed would produce a curved graph whose gradient becomes progressively steeper, not a straight line.
Option C is incorrect because an object at rest is represented by a horizontal line with zero gradient.
Option D is incorrect because decreasing speed would produce a curve or a line with a reducing gradient, not a straight line with constant slope.
common_misconceptions: Many students confuse a rising straight line with increasing speed. In reality, a straight line means the gradient is constant, so the speed is constant.
Some learners focus only on the increasing distance and forget that it is the gradient, not the height of the graph, that determines speed.
explanation.exam_tip:
For JAMB questions involving distance–time graphs, always examine the gradient.

• Constant gradient → Uniform speed.
• Zero gradient → Object at rest.
• Increasing gradient → Increasing speed.
• Decreasing gradient → Decreasing speed.
explanation.final_answer: A. It moves with uniform speed.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine walking so that you cover exactly the same distance every minute. If this happens throughout the journey, your speed never changes. A straight-line distance–time graph represents this type of motion, called uniform motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance–Time Graphs to learn how to interpret graph shapes, identify stationary periods, determine speed from gradients, and solve JAMB-style graph questions. question:
A warehouse robot is monitored using a distance–time graph. The graph begins at the origin, rises uniformly to a distance of 40 m in 8 s, remains horizontal from 8 s to 14 s, and then rises uniformly again until the robot reaches 85 m at 23 s.

What does the horizontal section of the graph between 8 s and 14 s indicate?

options.A: The robot is moving with constant speed.
options.C: The robot is stationary during the interval.
options.D: The robot is moving back towards its starting point.

answer_text: The robot is stationary during the interval.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the meaning of a horizontal section on a distance–time graph. You are required to interpret what happens to an object's motion when its recorded distance remains unchanged over a period of time.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes with time.

On this graph:

• Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis.
• Distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The gradient of the graph represents the object's speed.

If the graph is horizontal, its gradient is zero. A zero gradient means the distance is not changing with time. Therefore, the object is not moving and is said to be stationary.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the portion of the graph being described.

From 8 s to 14 s, the graph is horizontal.

Step 2: Interpret what a horizontal line means.

During this interval, the recorded distance remains constant at 40 m. Since the distance does not increase or decrease, the robot does not change its position.

Step 3: Relate this to speed.

Speed is the gradient of a distance–time graph.

Gradient = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

During the horizontal section:

Change in distance = 0 m

Therefore,

Speed = 0 ÷ (14 − 8) = 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

A speed of 0 m s⁻¹ means the robot is stationary.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because constant speed is represented by a straight line with a non-zero slope, not a horizontal line.
Option B is incorrect because acceleration refers to a change in speed. A horizontal distance–time graph indicates zero speed, not acceleration.
Option D is incorrect because moving back towards the starting point cannot be represented on a distance–time graph. Distance never decreases because it is the total path travelled.
common_misconceptions: Some students think a horizontal distance–time graph means the object is moving horizontally. In reality, it means the distance remains unchanged.
Others confuse a horizontal distance–time graph with zero distance. The object may still be some distance from its starting point while remaining at rest.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you see a horizontal section on a distance–time graph, immediately think of zero gradient, zero speed and an object at rest. JAMB frequently tests this concept by describing the graph in words rather than showing the actual graph.
explanation.final_answer: C. The robot is stationary during the interval.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine a delivery robot that stops beside a storage shelf for a few seconds before continuing its journey. Although time continues to pass, the robot's distance from its starting point does not change while it is stopped. This produces a horizontal line on a distance–time graph.

question:
A drone delivery company reviews the motion of one of its drones using a distance–time graph described below.

The graph starts at the origin and becomes progressively steeper as time increases. It does not contain any straight-line or horizontal section.

Which of the following best describes the motion of the drone?

options.A: The drone is moving with a constant speed throughout the journey.
options.B: The drone remains stationary throughout the journey.
options.C: The drone is moving with a speed that changes continuously during the journey.
options.D: The drone moves at a constant speed and then suddenly comes to rest.

answer_text: The drone is moving with a speed that changes continuously during the journey.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the shape of a distance–time graph. Specifically, you are required to recognise what a curved graph with an increasing gradient tells you about an object's motion.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The gradient of a distance–time graph represents the object's speed.

If the graph is a straight line, the gradient is constant, meaning the object moves with uniform speed.

If the graph is horizontal, the gradient is zero, meaning the object is stationary.

If the graph is curved, the gradient changes from one point to another. This means the speed is not constant. A graph that becomes progressively steeper indicates that the object's speed is increasing as time passes.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the type of graph described.

The graph is curved rather than a straight line.

Step 2: Recall the meaning of the gradient.

The gradient of a distance–time graph represents speed.

Step 3: Observe how the gradient changes.

The graph becomes progressively steeper, showing that the gradient increases with time.

Step 4: Interpret the motion.

Since the gradient is changing, the speed is also changing continuously. Therefore, the drone is not moving with uniform speed.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because constant speed is represented by a straight-line distance–time graph with a constant gradient.
Option B is incorrect because a stationary object is represented by a horizontal graph with zero gradient.
Option D is incorrect because the description contains no horizontal section to indicate that the drone stopped moving.
common_misconceptions: Some students confuse a curved graph with a straight graph and incorrectly assume the speed is constant.
Others focus on the increasing distance rather than the changing gradient, forgetting that speed depends on the slope of the graph.

A common mistake is to think every curved distance–time graph represents constant acceleration. The graph only shows that the speed is changing; it does not by itself specify how the acceleration behaves.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, always interpret the gradient instead of the height of a distance–time graph.

Remember:
• Straight line → constant speed.
• Horizontal line → object at rest.
• Curved line → changing speed.
• Steeper gradient → higher speed.
explanation.final_answer: C. The drone is moving with a speed that changes continuously during the journey.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding a bicycle and pedalling faster and faster. You cover more distance every second than before. On a distance–time graph, this produces a curve that becomes steeper. The increasing steepness shows that your speed is changing rather than remaining constant.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons and additional practice.

question: A railway monitoring system records the motion of a maintenance train using a distance-time graph. The graph starts at the origin and rises uniformly from 0 m to 90 m during the first 15 s. Which of the following represents the speed of the train during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the speed of an object from the gradient of a distance-time graph.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The gradient (slope) of a distance-time graph represents the speed of the object.

Speed tells us how fast an object covers distance and is given by:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

For a straight-line distance-time graph, the gradient remains constant, meaning the object moves with uniform speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Distance travelled = 90 m

Time taken = 15 s

Step 2: State the appropriate formula.

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time

Step 3: Substitute the values.

Speed = 90 ÷ 15

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

Speed = 6 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated speed is 6 m s⁻¹, which corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (4 m s⁻¹) underestimates the gradient of the graph and would correspond to a smaller distance covered in 15 s.
Option B (5 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect calculation of the graph's gradient.
Option D (7 m s⁻¹) overestimates the gradient and would require the train to cover more than 90 m in 15 s.
common_misconceptions: Confusing the gradient of a distance-time graph with acceleration instead of speed.
Using the graph height alone instead of calculating the change in distance divided by the change in time.
Reading the graph axes incorrectly by interchanging distance and time.
explanation.exam_tip:
For every straight-line distance-time graph, immediately think: Gradient = Speed.

Always calculate the change in distance divided by the corresponding change in time using the correct graph segment.
explanation.final_answer: C. 6 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation:
The train travels 90 m in 15 s.

Speed = Distance ÷ Time = 90 ÷ 15 = 6 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs to learn how to interpret distance-time graphs, calculate graph gradients, identify stationary intervals, distinguish uniform and non-uniform motion, and solve JAMB-style graph interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons, worked examples, and additional examination practice.

question:
A campus electric bus is monitored using a distance-time graph. The graph shows that during one section of its journey, the distance travelled increases uniformly from 48 m at 6 s to 120 m at 15 s.

What is the speed of the bus during this section of the graph?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to determine the speed of a campus electric bus from a particular section of a distance-time graph by calculating the gradient of that graph segment.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

For any straight-line section of a distance-time graph, the object moves with uniform speed.

The speed is obtained from the gradient of that section of the graph.

The gradient is calculated using:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

Only the values within the specified graph segment should be used. Do not use the total journey unless the question asks for the average speed of the entire trip.
explanation.definitions:
Distance is the total length of the path travelled by an object.

Time is the duration taken for the motion.

Speed is the distance travelled per unit time.

The gradient of a distance-time graph represents the speed of the moving object.
explanation.formula_introduction:
For a distance-time graph:

Speed = (Change in distance) ÷ (Change in time)

where:

• Change in distance = Final distance − Initial distance
• Change in time = Final time − Initial time
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial distance = 48 m
Final distance = 120 m
Initial time = 6 s
Final time = 15 s

Step 2: Calculate the change in distance.

Change in distance = 120 − 48 = 72 m

Step 3: Calculate the change in time.

Change in time = 15 − 6 = 9 s

Step 4: Apply the formula.

Speed = 72 ÷ 9 = 8 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct option is 8 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (6 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect calculation of the graph gradient.
Option C (10 m s⁻¹) is obtained if the distance or time interval is misread from the graph.
Option D (12 m s⁻¹) is too large and would result from using incorrect graph values or ignoring the specified graph segment.
explanation.common_misconceptions:
A common mistake is to use only the final distance and final time instead of the changes between the two points.

Another mistake is to read the graph height instead of calculating the slope.

Always subtract both the distance values and the corresponding time values before finding the speed.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks for the speed from a distance-time graph, immediately think of the graph's gradient. Carefully identify the two points on the required graph segment before performing the calculation.
explanation.final_answer: B. 8 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the bus has travelled an extra 72 m in 9 s during one part of its journey. Dividing the distance covered by the time taken gives 72 ÷ 9 = 8 m s⁻¹. Therefore, the bus moves at a constant speed of 8 m s⁻¹ during that section.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
During a cycling competition, officials analyse the distance-time graphs of Cyclist P and Cyclist Q over the same interval. The graph for Cyclist P has a constant gradient corresponding to a speed of 6 m s⁻¹, while the graph for Cyclist Q has a constant gradient corresponding to a speed of 9 m s⁻¹.

Which statement correctly compares the motions of the two cyclists?

options.A: Cyclist P is faster because the graph covers the same time interval.
options.B: Cyclist Q is faster because its distance-time graph has the steeper gradient.
options.C: Both cyclists move at the same speed because both graphs are straight lines.
options.D: Cyclist Q travels a shorter distance because its graph has a larger gradient.

answer_text: Cyclist Q is faster because its distance-time graph has the steeper gradient.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to compare the speeds of two objects by interpreting the gradients of their distance-time graphs rather than by reading the graph heights or the total time alone.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

The gradient of a distance-time graph represents speed.

Gradient is calculated as:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

A steeper straight-line graph means a greater gradient, and therefore a higher constant speed.

If two graphs are straight lines, both objects move with uniform speed, but the object with the steeper line is the faster one.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the speeds represented by the gradients.

Cyclist P has a speed of 6 m s⁻¹.

Cyclist Q has a speed of 9 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Compare the two speeds.

Since 9 m s⁻¹ is greater than 6 m s⁻¹, Cyclist Q is moving faster.

Step 3: Relate this to the graph.

A larger gradient means the graph rises more steeply, indicating that more distance is covered every second.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

The correct statement is that Cyclist Q is faster because its graph has the steeper gradient.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because speed depends on the graph's gradient, not simply on the length of the time interval.
Option C is incorrect because straight lines only indicate uniform motion. They do not necessarily indicate equal speeds unless the gradients are the same.
Option D is incorrect because a larger gradient indicates that more distance is covered per unit time, not less.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB questions involving distance-time graphs, always compare the slopes of the graph before comparing the graph heights. Remember:

• Steeper gradient → Higher speed.
• Horizontal line → Object at rest.
• Straight line → Uniform speed.
• Curved line → Changing speed.
explanation.final_answer: B. Cyclist Q is faster because its distance-time graph has the steeper gradient.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two cyclists starting together. If one cyclist's distance increases more quickly every second, the line on the distance-time graph becomes steeper. Since Cyclist Q covers 9 metres every second while Cyclist P covers only 6 metres every second, Cyclist Q is the faster cyclist.

question:
A logistics company monitors one of its delivery vehicles using a distance–time graph. The graph is described as follows:

• The vehicle starts from the loading bay.
• It travels uniformly from 0 m to 300 m during the first 25 s.
• It remains stationary for the next 15 s.
• It then travels uniformly from 300 m to 660 m during the next 20 s.

What is the average speed of the vehicle for the entire journey shown on the graph?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the average speed of a vehicle from a described distance–time graph containing both moving and stationary periods.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time. The gradient of any straight section represents the speed during that section. However, average speed over an entire journey is calculated using the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken, including any period during which the object is stationary.

The formula is:

Average speed = Total distance travelled ÷ Total time taken

where:
• Total distance travelled is the final distance reached.
• Total time taken includes both moving and resting intervals.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the total distance travelled.

The vehicle finishes at a distance of 660 m.

Therefore,
Total distance = 660 m.

Step 2: Determine the total time.

Moving time = 25 s + 20 s = 45 s.
Stationary time = 15 s.

Total time = 45 s + 15 s = 60 s.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

Average speed = Total distance ÷ Total time
= 660 m ÷ 60 s
= 11.0 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct option is 11.00 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (8.25 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect calculation of either the total distance or total time.
Option C (13.20 m s⁻¹) is obtained by dividing the total distance by only the first moving interval of 50 s or by ignoring part of the journey.
Option D (16.50 m s⁻¹) results from excluding the stationary period or using an incorrect total time.
common_misconceptions: Using only the time during which the vehicle was moving instead of the total journey time.
Adding individual speeds instead of calculating average speed from total distance and total time.
Confusing average speed with the speed on one section of the graph.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, average speed from a distance–time graph always uses the total distance travelled and the total elapsed time. Remember to include every stop shown by a horizontal section of the graph because time continues to pass even when the object is at rest.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a delivery van that stops briefly before continuing its journey. Although it is not moving during the stop, time is still passing. Therefore, the average speed must be calculated using the entire journey time, not just the time spent moving.

Average speed = 660 m ÷ 60 s = 11.00 m s⁻¹.

question:
A delivery van follows a route represented by a distance–time graph described as follows:

• From 0 s to 12 s, the distance increases uniformly from 0 m to 96 m.
• From 12 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 96 m.
• From 18 s to 30 s, the distance increases uniformly from 96 m to 216 m.

What is the average speed of the delivery van for the entire 30 s journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to analyse a multi-stage distance–time graph described in words and determine the average speed for the complete journey.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time. The gradient of each straight-line section represents the speed during that interval. However, to calculate the average speed for the whole journey, it is not necessary to calculate the speed of each section individually.

Average speed is defined as the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken.

The formula is:

Average speed = Total distance travelled ÷ Total time taken.
explanation.definitions:
Distance is the total length of the path travelled by an object regardless of direction.

Average speed is the ratio of the total distance travelled to the total time taken for the journey.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: A horizontal section of a distance–time graph means the object is stationary because the distance does not change with time. Although the object is not moving during that interval, the time spent at rest must still be included when calculating the average speed.
steps:
Step 1: Determine the total distance travelled.

The graph ends at a distance of 216 m.

Therefore, total distance = 216 m.

Step 2: Determine the total time taken.

The journey begins at 0 s and ends at 30 s.

Therefore, total time = 30 s.

Step 3: Apply the average speed formula.

Average speed = Total distance ÷ Total time

= 216 ÷ 30

= 7.2 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Compare with the options.

The calculated value is 7.2 m s⁻¹, which corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (5.6 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation of the total distance or total time.
Option C (8.0 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it ignores the stationary interval and effectively assumes the van was moving throughout the journey.
Option D (18.0 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it is obtained by using an inappropriate graph segment rather than the complete journey.
explanation.common_misconceptions: Many candidates calculate the average speed using only the periods when the vehicle is moving. This is incorrect because average speed must include every part of the journey, including any time spent at rest.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB questions involving distance–time graphs, first identify the total distance at the end of the graph and the total elapsed time. Use these values directly when asked for the average speed of the entire journey.
explanation.final_answer: B. 7.2 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation:
The van finishes 216 m from the starting point after 30 s. Average speed simply means the total distance divided by the total time.

216 ÷ 30 = 7.2 m s⁻¹.

The period during which the van stopped is included because time continued to pass.

question:
A factory conveyor transports packaged items along a production line. Its distance-time graph is described as follows:

• From 0 s to 8 s, the graph rises uniformly.
• From 8 s to 14 s, the graph continues to rise but with a less steep straight-line slope.
• From 14 s to 20 s, the graph becomes horizontal.

Which statement best describes the motion of the conveyor throughout the journey?

options.A: The conveyor moves with constant speed at first, then with a lower constant speed, and finally comes to rest.
options.B: The conveyor accelerates continuously before stopping.
options.C: The conveyor moves with increasing speed throughout the journey before stopping.
options.D: The conveyor travels at the same constant speed throughout the journey and then suddenly reverses direction.

answer_text: The conveyor moves with constant speed at first, then with a lower constant speed, and finally comes to rest.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret how changes in the slope of a distance-time graph describe changes in an object's speed and state of motion.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how distance changes with time.

The gradient (slope) of the graph represents speed.

• A straight line with a constant slope indicates uniform (constant) speed.
• A steeper straight line represents a higher constant speed.
• A less steep straight line represents a lower constant speed.
• A horizontal line has zero gradient, meaning the object is stationary because its distance no longer changes.

Since a distance-time graph does not show direction, it cannot indicate that an object has reversed its motion.
steps:
Step 1: Interpret the first section.

From 0 s to 8 s, the graph rises uniformly with a straight-line slope. This indicates that the conveyor moves with constant speed.

Step 2: Interpret the second section.

From 8 s to 14 s, the graph is still a straight line but is less steep. Since the slope has decreased, the conveyor is still moving uniformly but at a lower constant speed.

Step 3: Interpret the final section.

From 14 s to 20 s, the graph is horizontal. The gradient is zero, so the conveyor is no longer moving.

Step 4: Select the correct description.

The conveyor moves at a constant speed initially, then continues at a lower constant speed, and finally remains at rest.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because acceleration would require the slope to change continuously. The graph consists of straight-line sections with constant slopes.
Option C is incorrect because the slope becomes less steep, indicating a reduction rather than an increase in speed.
Option D is incorrect because the graph never shows reversal of direction. A distance-time graph represents total distance travelled and does not indicate direction.
common_misconceptions: Assuming that a less steep line means the object has stopped.
Confusing the height of the graph with speed instead of using the slope.
Thinking a horizontal distance-time graph means the distance is zero rather than constant.
Believing a distance-time graph can show a change in direction.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever interpreting a distance-time graph, examine the slope of each section individually. Compare their steepness rather than their height. A horizontal section always represents zero speed.
explanation.final_answer: A. The conveyor moves with constant speed at first, then with a lower constant speed, and finally comes to rest.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a trolley. At first you push it steadily, then you slow your walking pace, and eventually you stop pushing altogether. On a distance-time graph, this appears as a steep straight line, followed by a less steep straight line, and finally a horizontal line.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs to learn how graph gradients represent speed, how to identify stationary periods, and how to interpret multi-stage journeys. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style worked examples and graphical interpretation exercises.

question:
An engineering laboratory compares four distance–time graphs obtained from different mobile inspection robots. Each graph is a straight line, indicating uniform motion.

Graph I: Distance increases by 40 m in 10 s.
Graph II: Distance increases by 72 m in 18 s.
Graph III: Distance increases by 96 m in 16 s.
Graph IV: Distance increases by 75 m in 15 s.

Which graph represents the robot moving with the greatest uniform speed?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to compare the gradients of straight-line distance–time graphs. Since the graphs are described numerically, you must determine which graph has the greatest gradient and hence the greatest speed.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how distance changes with time. The gradient (slope) of a straight-line distance–time graph represents the object's speed.

For uniform motion:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

The steeper the graph, the greater the speed.

A common mistake is to compare only the distances travelled or only the times taken. Both quantities must be considered together by calculating the gradient.
steps:
Step 1: Recall that speed is the gradient of a distance–time graph.

Speed = Distance ÷ Time.

Step 2: Calculate the speed for each graph.

Graph I = 40 ÷ 10 = 4 m s⁻¹.

Graph II = 72 ÷ 18 = 4 m s⁻¹.

Graph III = 96 ÷ 16 = 6 m s⁻¹.

Graph IV = 75 ÷ 15 = 5 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Compare the calculated speeds.

Graph III has the highest speed of 6 m s⁻¹.
Step 4: Since the greatest speed corresponds to the steepest distance–time graph, Graph III is the correct choice.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Graph I has a speed of only 4 m s⁻¹, which is less than Graph III.
Option B is incorrect because Graph II also has a speed of 4 m s⁻¹ and is not the steepest graph.
Option D is incorrect because Graph IV has a speed of 5 m s⁻¹, which is greater than Graphs I and II but still less than Graph III.
common_misconceptions: Choosing the graph with the greatest distance instead of the greatest gradient.
Assuming that a longer time automatically means a higher speed.
Comparing distances without dividing by the corresponding times.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks you to identify the steepest distance–time graph, calculate the gradient if the values are provided. The graph with the greatest distance divided by the corresponding time represents the highest speed.
explanation.final_answer: C. Graph III
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine four robots travelling for different distances and times. The fastest robot is not necessarily the one that travels the longest distance but the one that covers the most distance every second. Graph III covers 96 m in 16 s, giving 6 m s⁻¹, which is higher than the speeds of the other robots.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance–Time Graphs to learn how gradients represent speed, how to identify the steepest graph, and how to solve JAMB-style graph interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and examination-focused practice.

question:
An airport ground vehicle is monitored using a distance-time graph described as follows:

• From 0 s to 12 s, the graph rises uniformly from 0 m to 84 m.
• From 12 s to 20 s, the graph is horizontal at 84 m.
• From 20 s to 32 s, the graph rises uniformly from 84 m to 180 m.

For how long was the vehicle stationary?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the period during which an object is at rest by interpreting a distance-time graph described in words.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical axis.

The gradient of the graph represents speed.

When the graph is horizontal, the distance does not change even though time continues to pass. Since no additional distance is covered, the object is stationary and its speed is zero.
explanation.definitions: A stationary object is one whose position remains unchanged with time. On a distance-time graph, this is represented by a horizontal line because the distance remains constant.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Every section of a distance-time graph tells a story about the motion of an object.

• An upward straight line indicates uniform speed.
• A steeper line indicates a higher speed.
• A horizontal line indicates that the object has stopped moving.

To determine the duration of rest, identify the horizontal section and subtract its starting time from its ending time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the horizontal portion of the graph.

The graph is horizontal from 12 s to 20 s.

Step 2: Calculate the duration of the stationary period.

Duration = Ending time − Starting time

= 20 s − 12 s

= 8 s.

Step 3: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct option is 8 s.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (6 s) is incorrect because it underestimates the duration of the horizontal section.
Option C (12 s) is incorrect because it incorrectly uses the duration of one of the moving intervals instead of the stationary interval.
Option D (20 s) is incorrect because it confuses the elapsed time from the beginning of the motion with the duration of rest.
explanation.common_misconceptions: Many candidates wrongly believe that a horizontal line means the object has covered zero distance. In reality, it means the distance remains constant, indicating that the object is temporarily at rest.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB questions, whenever a distance-time graph contains a horizontal section, immediately recognise that the object is stationary during that interval. The duration of rest is obtained by subtracting the starting time from the ending time of the horizontal segment.
explanation.final_answer: B. 8 s
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the airport vehicle stops to allow an aircraft to pass. During that period, time keeps increasing but the vehicle does not move, so the distance stays at 84 m. The stop lasts from 12 s to 20 s, giving a duration of 8 s.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs to learn how to interpret graph segments, identify stationary intervals, calculate speed from gradients, and solve JAMB-style graphical interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided examples and practice.

question:
A metro train is monitored using a distance–time graph described as follows:

• From 0 s to 12 s, the train moves uniformly from 0 m to 180 m.
• From 12 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 180 m.
• From 18 s to 30 s, the train moves uniformly from 180 m to 420 m.

What is the total distance travelled by the train during the entire journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the total distance travelled from a distance–time graph described in words. You must interpret each stage of the journey correctly before finding the final distance travelled.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal (x) axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical (y) axis.

The vertical value at any point on the graph represents the total distance already covered.

A horizontal section of the graph indicates that the object is stationary. During this period, time increases but the distance travelled remains unchanged.

To obtain the total distance travelled, read the final distance value on the graph. Since this is a distance–time graph, the final vertical reading already represents the cumulative distance covered.
steps:
Step 1: Interpret the first stage.

From 0 s to 12 s, the train travels from 0 m to 180 m.

Distance covered = 180 m.

Step 2: Interpret the second stage.

From 12 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 180 m.

The train is at rest, so no additional distance is covered.

Total distance remains 180 m.

Step 3: Interpret the third stage.

From 18 s to 30 s, the graph rises from 180 m to 420 m.

The train covers an additional 240 m during this interval.

Step 4: Determine the total distance travelled.

The final reading on the distance–time graph is 420 m.

Therefore, the total distance travelled during the entire journey is 420 m.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it represents the distance travelled before the train stopped and ignores the final stage of the journey.
Option B is incorrect because it is only the additional distance covered after the stop, not the total distance travelled.

Option D is incorrect because it results from adding cumulative graph readings (180 m + 420 m), which double-counts part of the journey. On a distance–time graph, the final reading already gives the total distance travelled.
common_misconceptions: Adding cumulative distance readings together instead of reading the final distance value.
Assuming a horizontal graph means the distance becomes zero.
Confusing total distance travelled with the distance covered during only the last stage of motion.
explanation.exam_tip: For every distance–time graph, remember that the final vertical reading gives the total distance travelled. A horizontal section simply indicates that the object is temporarily at rest; it does not reduce the distance already covered.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine checking the odometer of a vehicle. When the vehicle stops briefly, the odometer does not return to zero—it simply remains unchanged until the vehicle starts moving again. Likewise, the final distance reading on a distance–time graph shows the total distance travelled.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance–Time Graphs to learn how to interpret graph sections, identify stationary intervals, calculate speed from gradients, determine total distance travelled, and solve JAMB-style graphical motion questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and practice exercises.

question:
A motion sensor in a physics laboratory records the movement of a trolley along a straight track. The distance-time graph obtained is described as follows:

• From 0 s to 10 s, the graph rises uniformly from 0 m to 50 m.
• From 10 s to 16 s, the graph is horizontal at 50 m.
• From 16 s to 28 s, the graph rises uniformly from 50 m to 134 m.

Which conclusion is most consistent with the experimental graph?

options.A: The trolley moved with the same speed throughout the experiment.
options.B: The trolley was stationary between 10 s and 16 s before moving again at a higher uniform speed.
options.C: The trolley accelerated continuously throughout the experiment.
options.D: The trolley reversed its direction between 10 s and 16 s.

answer_text: The trolley was stationary between 10 s and 16 s before moving again at a higher uniform speed.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret an experimentally obtained distance-time graph by relating different sections of the graph to the actual motion of the trolley.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical axis.

The gradient of a distance-time graph represents speed.

A straight line with a constant gradient indicates uniform speed.

A horizontal line has zero gradient, meaning the object is at rest.

A steeper straight line represents a greater uniform speed.

Because a distance-time graph records total distance travelled, it does not indicate the direction of motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first section.

From 0 s to 10 s, the distance increases uniformly from 0 m to 50 m.

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time = 50 m ÷ 10 s = 5 m s⁻¹.

The trolley therefore moved with uniform speed.

Step 2: Examine the second section.

From 10 s to 16 s, the graph is horizontal at 50 m.

The distance does not change during this interval.

Therefore, the trolley was stationary.

Step 3: Examine the third section.

From 16 s to 28 s, the distance increases from 50 m to 134 m.

Change in distance = 84 m.

Change in time = 12 s.

Speed = 84 m ÷ 12 s = 7 m s⁻¹.

The trolley again moved with uniform speed, but this speed is greater than the initial 5 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Select the correct conclusion.

The experiment shows three stages:

• Uniform motion.
• A period of rest.
• Uniform motion at a higher speed.

Therefore, Option B is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the first and third sections have different gradients, showing different uniform speeds.
Option C is incorrect because continuous acceleration would produce a curved distance-time graph rather than separate straight-line sections.
Option D is incorrect because a distance-time graph does not indicate reversal of direction, and the distance never decreases.
common_misconceptions: Confusing a horizontal section with zero distance instead of zero motion.
Assuming a steeper graph represents acceleration rather than greater speed.
Thinking a distance-time graph can directly show a change in direction.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, always examine the gradient of each section separately. A horizontal section means the object is at rest, while a steeper straight section indicates a higher constant speed, not acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: B. The trolley was stationary between 10 s and 16 s before moving again at a higher uniform speed.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a trolley in a laboratory. You first push it steadily, then stop pushing for a while so it remains still, and finally push it again faster than before. The graph described matches exactly this sequence of events.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs for a complete lesson on interpreting graph gradients, identifying stationary intervals, calculating speed from graph segments, and solving JAMB-style graphical reasoning questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and practice exercises.

question:
During a Physics practical, a motion sensor produced the following distance-time graph for a warehouse robot:

• From 0 s to 12 s, the graph rises uniformly from 0 m to 48 m.
• From 12 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 48 m.
• From 18 s to 28 s, the graph rises uniformly from 48 m to 108 m.

A student concluded that the robot moved with uniform speed throughout the experiment because the graph contains straight-line sections.

Which observation correctly identifies the student's error?

options.A: The student ignored the horizontal section, which represents a period when the robot was stationary.
options.B: The student assumed the graph represented displacement instead of distance.
options.C: The student failed to recognise that the vertical axis should represent time.
options.D: The student assumed that every straight-line graph represents uniformly accelerated motion.

answer_text: The student ignored the horizontal section, which represents a period when the robot was stationary.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to identify and explain a common error made when interpreting a distance-time graph. You are required to determine why the student's conclusion is incorrect.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

On such a graph:

• A straight line with a constant positive slope indicates uniform speed.
• A horizontal line indicates that the distance does not change with time, meaning the object is at rest.
• Different sections of the graph must be interpreted separately.

A graph containing several straight-line segments does not necessarily represent uniform speed throughout the entire motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first section.

From 0 s to 12 s, the robot travels from 0 m to 48 m along a straight line. This indicates motion at a constant speed.

Step 2: Examine the second section.

From 12 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 48 m. Since the distance remains unchanged, the robot is stationary during this interval and its speed is 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Examine the final section.

From 18 s to 28 s, the graph rises uniformly again, showing that the robot resumes motion at another constant speed.

Step 4: Evaluate the student's conclusion.

The student incorrectly treated the graph as though every part represented continuous motion. The stationary interval interrupts the motion, so the robot did not move with uniform speed throughout the experiment.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

The student's mistake was failing to recognise the meaning of the horizontal section. Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the interpretation of the horizontal section would remain the same whether the graph represented distance or displacement. The student's main mistake concerns the stationary interval.
Option C is incorrect because, in a distance-time graph, time is plotted on the horizontal axis, not the vertical axis.
Option D is incorrect because a straight line on a distance-time graph indicates constant speed, not uniformly accelerated motion.
explanation.common_misconception: Many candidates believe that once a graph contains straight-line sections, the object must have moved with uniform speed throughout the journey. In reality, each section of the graph should be interpreted independently. A horizontal section always represents no motion.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever interpreting a distance-time graph, inspect each segment separately. A positive constant slope indicates uniform speed, while a horizontal segment indicates that the object is at rest. Never base your conclusion on only one part of the graph.
explanation.final_answer: A. The student ignored the horizontal section, which represents a period when the robot was stationary.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine a robot walking, stopping for a while, and then walking again. Although both moving parts occur at constant speeds, the stop means the robot did not move continuously at one uniform speed throughout the experiment. The flat part of the graph tells you the robot was resting.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs to learn how to interpret graph segments, identify stationary intervals, calculate speed from graph gradients, and solve JAMB-style graphical reasoning questions. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused practice.

question:
A GPS tracking system records the movement of a field survey vehicle using a distance–time graph described below.

The graph starts at the origin and rises uniformly from 0 m to 160 m during the first 20 s. It then remains horizontal for the next 10 s before rising uniformly from 160 m to 320 m during the final 20 s.

Which of the following statements correctly interprets the motion shown by the graph?

options.A: The vehicle moved with the same uniform speed before and after the stop, and remained stationary during the horizontal section.
options.B: The vehicle accelerated continuously throughout the journey because the total distance increased.
options.C: The vehicle travelled at a higher speed after the stop because the final distance was greater.
options.D: The horizontal section indicates that the vehicle was moving with zero acceleration but non-zero speed.

answer_text: The vehicle moved with the same uniform speed before and after the stop, and remained stationary during the horizontal section.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the different sections of a distance–time graph and determine what each part reveals about the motion of an object.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

The horizontal axis represents time, while the vertical axis represents distance.

The gradient of the graph represents speed.

A straight sloping line indicates uniform speed because the distance increases by equal amounts in equal intervals of time.

A horizontal line has zero gradient, meaning the distance does not change with time. Therefore, the object is stationary during that interval.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first section.

The vehicle travels from 0 m to 160 m in 20 s.

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time = 160 ÷ 20 = 8 m s⁻¹.

This is uniform motion because the graph is a straight line.

Step 2: Examine the middle section.

The graph is horizontal for 10 s.

Since the distance remains constant at 160 m, the vehicle is not moving during this period.

Its speed is 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Examine the final section.

The vehicle travels from 160 m to 320 m in 20 s.

Speed = (320 − 160) ÷ 20 = 160 ÷ 20 = 8 m s⁻¹.

The speed is the same as in the first section.

Step 4: Compare the two moving sections.

Both gradients are equal, so the vehicle moved with the same uniform speed before and after the stop.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a straight distance–time graph represents constant speed, not continuous acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because a greater final distance does not necessarily mean a higher speed. Speed depends on the gradient, which is the same in both moving sections.
Option D is incorrect because a horizontal distance–time graph represents zero speed, not motion with non-zero speed.
common_misconceptions: Confusing the height of the graph with speed instead of using its gradient.
Assuming that an object is still moving whenever the graph is above the time axis.
Believing that a larger total distance automatically means a greater speed.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB questions involving distance–time graphs, always interpret the slope before looking at the total distance. Equal slopes represent equal speeds, while a horizontal section always indicates that the object is at rest.
explanation.final_answer: A. The vehicle moved with the same uniform speed before and after the stop, and remained stationary during the horizontal section.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine driving to a destination, stopping briefly at a traffic checkpoint, and then continuing at exactly the same speed as before. On a distance–time graph, the stop appears as a horizontal line, while the equal speeds before and after the stop appear as straight lines with the same steepness.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance–Time Graphs to learn how to interpret graph segments, identify stationary intervals, calculate speed from gradients, and solve JAMB-style graphical interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and revision exercises.

question:
A smart transportation centre monitors the movement of an autonomous shuttle using a distance-time graph described below.

• From 0 s to 10 s, the shuttle travels uniformly from 0 m to 80 m.
• From 10 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 80 m.
• From 18 s to 30 s, the shuttle moves uniformly from 80 m to 224 m.

What are the speeds of the shuttle during the first and third stages respectively?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret different sections of a distance-time graph and calculate the speed during each stage by finding the gradient of the graph.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

The gradient or slope of a distance-time graph represents the speed of the object.

Speed is calculated using:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.

A straight sloping line indicates uniform speed, while a horizontal line indicates that the object is stationary because the distance does not change.
explanation.definitions:
Distance is the total length of the path travelled by an object.

Time is the duration taken for the motion.

Speed is the rate at which distance is covered and is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
steps:
Step 1: Determine the speed during the first stage.

Distance covered = 80 m − 0 m = 80 m.

Time taken = 10 s − 0 s = 10 s.

Speed = 80 ÷ 10 = 8 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Interpret the second stage.

The graph is horizontal from 10 s to 18 s.

The shuttle remains at 80 m throughout this interval, so it is stationary and its speed is 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Determine the speed during the third stage.

Distance covered = 224 m − 80 m = 144 m.

Time taken = 30 s − 18 s = 12 s.

Speed = 144 ÷ 12 = 12 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Compare the calculated values with the options.

The speeds are 8 m s⁻¹ during the first stage and 12 m s⁻¹ during the third stage.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the first-stage speed is 8 m s⁻¹, not 10 m s⁻¹.
Option C is incorrect because the third-stage speed is 12 m s⁻¹, not 10 m s⁻¹.
Option D is incorrect because it reverses the correct speeds for the first and third stages.
explanation.common_misconceptions:
Some candidates calculate speed using the total journey instead of the specified graph segment.

Others mistakenly use the graph height instead of the change in distance, or forget that a horizontal section represents zero speed.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a distance-time graph has several stages, calculate the gradient of each stage separately. Never use the total distance or total time unless the question specifically asks for the average speed of the entire journey.
explanation.final_answer: A. 8 m s⁻¹ and 12 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation: The shuttle first covers 80 m in 10 s, giving a speed of 8 m s⁻¹. It then stops briefly. Finally, it covers another 144 m in 12 s, giving a speed of 12 m s⁻¹. Hence, the correct pair of speeds is 8 m s⁻¹ and 12 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Motion Graphs → Distance-Time Graphs to learn how graph gradients represent speed, how to analyse multi-stage journeys, identify stationary intervals and solve JAMB-style graphical interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and examination practice.

question:
Engineers monitoring a high-speed rail service describe a distance-time graph as follows:

• From 0 s to 12 s, the train travels from 0 m to 180 m.
• From 12 s to 20 s, the graph is horizontal.
• From 20 s to 28 s, the train travels from 180 m to 380 m.
• From 28 s to 40 s, the train travels from 380 m to 560 m.

During which interval was the train moving at the highest speed?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the fastest part of a journey from a described distance-time graph by comparing the gradients of the graph segments.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled changes with time.

The gradient of a distance-time graph represents speed.

A steeper gradient means a higher speed, while a horizontal line has zero gradient and therefore represents an object at rest.

To compare speeds, calculate the gradient of each moving section using:

Speed = Change in distance ÷ Change in time.
explanation.definitions:
Distance is the total length of the path travelled by an object.

Time is the duration of motion.

Speed is the rate at which distance is covered.

Gradient is the slope of a graph and, for a distance-time graph, it is equal to the speed.
steps:
Step 1: Calculate the speed from 0 s to 12 s.

Distance covered = 180 m.
Time taken = 12 s.

Speed = 180 ÷ 12 = 15 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Calculate the speed from 12 s to 20 s.

The graph is horizontal, so the train does not move.

Speed = 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Calculate the speed from 20 s to 28 s.

Distance covered = 380 − 180 = 200 m.
Time taken = 8 s.

Speed = 200 ÷ 8 = 25 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Calculate the speed from 28 s to 40 s.

Distance covered = 560 − 380 = 180 m.
Time taken = 12 s.

Speed = 180 ÷ 12 = 15 m s⁻¹.

Step 5: Compare the speeds.

0–12 s = 15 m s⁻¹
12–20 s = 0 m s⁻¹
20–28 s = 25 m s⁻¹
28–40 s = 15 m s⁻¹

The greatest speed is 25 m s⁻¹ during the interval from 20 s to 28 s.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the speed during this interval is 15 m s⁻¹, which is less than 25 m s⁻¹.
Option B is incorrect because the horizontal graph indicates the train is stationary, so its speed is zero.
Option D is incorrect because the speed is again 15 m s⁻¹, not the maximum speed.
common_misconceptions: Choosing the interval with the greatest total distance instead of the greatest gradient.
Assuming the highest point on the graph represents the highest speed.
Ignoring the time taken and comparing only the distances travelled.
Forgetting that a horizontal section represents zero speed.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, when asked for the fastest interval on a distance-time graph, always compare the gradients rather than the total distances. The steepest section of the graph represents the highest speed.
explanation.final_answer: C. 20 s to 28 s
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine walking along four parts of a journey. The fastest part is not necessarily the longest one but the part where you cover the most distance in the shortest time. Here, the train covers 200 m in only 8 s, giving the greatest speed of 25 m s⁻¹. Therefore, the fastest interval is from 20 s to 28 s.

question:
During an engineering design meeting, four engineers discuss the interpretation of a distance–time graph describing the motion of an inspection robot.

Statement I: A horizontal section of the graph indicates that the robot is stationary during that interval.

Statement II: A steeper straight-line section represents a higher uniform speed than a less steep straight-line section.

Statement III: The gradient of a distance–time graph represents the acceleration of the robot.

Statement IV: A curved distance–time graph may indicate that the robot is moving with changing speed.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to evaluate several statements about a distance–time graph and identify those that correctly describe the motion of an object.
explanation.principle:
A distance–time graph shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical axis.

The gradient of a distance–time graph gives the speed of the object because speed is the rate at which distance changes with time.

A horizontal graph has zero gradient, meaning the distance does not change with time and the object is at rest.

A straight-line graph with constant gradient represents uniform speed.

A steeper gradient corresponds to a greater speed.

A curved graph indicates that the speed is changing because the gradient is not constant.

Acceleration describes the rate of change of velocity with time and is not represented directly by the gradient of a distance–time graph.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

A horizontal section means the distance remains constant while time increases.

The object is therefore stationary.

Statement I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

The steeper the straight-line graph, the larger the gradient.

Since gradient represents speed, a steeper straight line indicates a higher uniform speed.

Statement II is correct.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

The gradient of a distance–time graph represents speed, not acceleration.

Acceleration is obtained from the gradient of a velocity–time graph.

Statement III is incorrect.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

A curved distance–time graph has a changing gradient.

Since the gradient represents speed, the speed is changing.

Statement IV is correct.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statements I, II and IV are correct, while Statement III is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement III, which wrongly identifies the gradient as acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because it omits Statement I, which correctly describes a stationary object on a horizontal graph.
Option D is incorrect because Statement III is false. Since one incorrect statement is included, the entire combination is incorrect.
common_misconceptions: Confusing speed with acceleration when interpreting graph gradients.
Thinking a horizontal distance–time graph means the object has travelled zero distance rather than remaining at the same distance.
Believing the height of the graph indicates speed instead of the slope.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, always identify the type of graph before interpreting its gradient.

Remember:

• Distance–time graph → Gradient = Speed.
• Velocity–time graph → Gradient = Acceleration.

Also note that a steeper distance–time graph indicates greater speed, while a horizontal section indicates the object is at rest.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine watching a robot move.

If its distance does not change, it has stopped.

If its distance increases steadily, it is moving at a constant speed.

If the graph becomes steeper, it is moving faster.

If the graph bends, its speed is changing.

Only Statement III is wrong because the slope of a distance–time graph gives speed, not acceleration.

Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional graph-based practice.

question:
Engineers monitoring an autonomous mining vehicle obtain the following description of its distance-time graph:

• From 0 s to 10 s, the graph rises uniformly from 0 m to 60 m.
• From 10 s to 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 60 m.
• From 18 s to 30 s, the graph rises uniformly from 60 m to 180 m.

A trainee engineer makes the following statements about the vehicle's motion:

I. The vehicle remained stationary between 10 s and 18 s.
II. The vehicle moved faster during the final stage than during the first stage.
III. The total distance travelled by the vehicle at the end of the journey was 180 m.
IV. The horizontal section of the graph indicates that the vehicle reversed its direction.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret a multi-stage distance-time graph by identifying periods of rest, comparing speeds using graph gradients, determining the total distance travelled and recognising what a horizontal section of the graph represents.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the total distance travelled by an object changes with time.

Time is plotted on the horizontal axis, while distance is plotted on the vertical axis.

The gradient of a distance-time graph represents speed.

A straight sloping line represents uniform speed.

A horizontal line means the distance remains constant with time, indicating that the object is stationary.

Unlike a displacement-time graph, a distance-time graph does not indicate the direction of motion. Therefore, it cannot be used to conclude that an object has reversed direction.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

Between 10 s and 18 s, the graph is horizontal at 60 m. Since the distance does not change during this interval, the vehicle is at rest.

Statement I is correct.

Step 2: Compare the speeds in the first and final stages.

First stage speed = change in distance ÷ change in time = 60 m ÷ 10 s = 6 m s⁻¹.

Final stage speed = (180 m − 60 m) ÷ (30 s − 18 s) = 120 m ÷ 12 s = 10 m s⁻¹.

Since 10 m s⁻¹ is greater than 6 m s⁻¹, the vehicle moved faster during the final stage.

Statement II is correct.

Step 3: Determine the total distance travelled.

At the end of the journey, the graph reaches 180 m. Therefore, the total distance travelled is 180 m.

Statement III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

A horizontal section means there is no change in distance with time. It indicates that the vehicle is stationary, not that it reversed direction.

Statement IV is incorrect.

Step 5: Select the correct combination.

Statements I, II and III are correct, while Statement IV is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Statement II and Statement III are both correct, while Statement IV is false.
Option C is incorrect because it includes Statement IV, which wrongly interprets a horizontal distance-time graph as a reversal of direction.
Option D is incorrect because it also includes Statement IV, making the combination incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip: When interpreting a distance-time graph, always remember that the gradient represents speed, not acceleration. A horizontal section means the object is stationary. Also, because the graph represents distance rather than displacement, it does not provide information about direction of motion.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and III only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of a mining vehicle carrying materials.

It moves, stops for loading, and then continues at a higher speed.

The flat part of the graph shows that it stopped moving, not that it went backwards.

The steeper final line means it travelled faster than before.

So only Statements I, II and III are correct.
